etymology.description: 好(好) (hǎo, "good") + 久(久) (jiǔ, "long time") + 不(不) (bù, "not") + 见(見) (jiàn, "see") combine to create the phrase meaning "long time no see."
etymology.interpretation: The phrase literally translates to 'good long time not see.' 好(好) (hǎo, "good") here serves as an intensifier, emphasizing 久(久) (jiǔ, "long time"). Together, they set up the context for time passing. Combining with 不(不) (bù, "not") + 见(見) (jiàn, "see"), the phrase directly conveys the idea of not seeing someone for a significant duration, epitomizing a common greeting after an extended separation.

examples.chinese: 好久不见(好久不見)，你最近怎么样(你最近怎麼樣)？
examples.english: Long time no see, how have you been recently?

examples.chinese: 我们在街上偶遇了(我們在街上偶遇了)，真是好久不见(真是好久不見)！
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen zài jiē shàng ǒuyù le, zhēnshì hǎojiǔ bújiàn!
examples.english: We bumped into each other on the street, it's really been a long time!

examples.chinese: 见到你真的很高兴(見到你真的很高興)，好久不见(好久不見)！
examples.pinyin: Jiàndào nǐ zhēnde hěn gāoxìng, hǎojiǔ bújiàn!
examples.english: I'm really happy to see you, long time no see!
